speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Ruth Keller. The interviewer is Louise [? Barbaro. ?] It is March 13, 1995. Ruth, we left off on tape number four with you saying you wanted nothing to do with your-- some of your relatives in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it's not-- not on my relatives. I meant the young people who came with me. We-- I thought we were all-- well, this-- these particular relatives, I definitely wanted nothing to do with them. But that's one thing. But I also found that being here, it was also very, very strange to me. And once having learned English, I found that the people with whom I came, and who kept on coming, that we were all rather neurotic. I didn't put it in those terms. Because I really, at that time, did not say neurotic. But I thought that if I'm here, I have to give it a best shot. And I was successful at work. I liked my work. I made friends. I-- I began to like everything about living in-- in New York. And of course, I did keep in touch. I did keep in touch with my-- the people from the OSE, and from Chabannes, who had come. But I found I had more fun going out with Americans. You know, I had American boyfriends. And it was a whole different world-- and was sort of learning as I went. And I only remember still being confused, though and not really wanting to speak about my parents, because it was much too hurtful. It was still something that the activity, the going, the-- the working, was all cutting that-- cutting that away. I used to read the papers and look for names, and always lurking in the back and looking. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And how neurotic I was myself, I can't tell. I mean, that's impossible to tell, but I must have been pretty much so. And I would play tennis until I dropped. I would go swimming-- anything to-- however, whoever-- whoever would listen, I would tell the story. Whereas some of my friends just certainly, certainly, just turned a page and left it behind, as though it didn't exist. And that I could not understand, and I still, to this day, cannot understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had two children. My older son-- now it's difficult. My older son, Joseph, was born in 1954. He was named after my father. And my younger son, Michael, whom you will meet, was born in 1956. I wanted children more than anything else. I had dreamed-- it was it was so odd. I had dreamed of having two boys. So I remember when the doctor said to me, OK, Ruth, wake up with my first child. It's a boy, she said. I answered her, I know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you couldn't know that. I said, yeah, I know that? And I had dreamed about that. And I was extremely happy. It was the happiest thing in my life, was to have my two boys. And my husband was a principal. And of course, I stopped working when I was pregnant with my first son. And then we moved to a little house in Little Neck. And then my second child was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't particularly care for this neighborhood, because I then realized that I was in a Catholic neighborhood. And at that point in time, I-- you know, I was-- I'm still you know, shell shock-- shell shocked, so to speak. And we moved to Great Neck, because the schools, and so on, and so forth. And the children went to school. And I started going back to school. I-- well, I first worked for Berlitz, teaching French. And then I got a job with an independent school in Locust Valley, teaching French. And I got my bachelor's and my master's degree. And when my older son was in his early teens, it was discovered that he had kyphosis, which is a curvature of the spine. My older son was a very sensitive boy, a very-- he was sensitive, very talented, and perhaps more aware of himself and of his artistic leanings, fighting against the artistic leanings, anyway. His ambition was to go to Annapolis, and to join the Navy. And when he was not quite 14, we had gone to an orthopedist. I had finally gone to an orthopedist. This was diagnosed. It was too late-- that spurt of growth. It was too late to correct it. Nowadays, you can correct this. In those days, you could not. And the orthopedist told him, well, there is a very good aspect to that. That was during the Vietnam War. You will never be drafted. You are ineligible. And he became extremely distraught. He started to say that it was a-- God's punishment. Because those whom God wants to punish, he makes cripples. And it was just downhill after that. He became more and more despondent. He-- we tried everything we knew. And when he was 26, he committed suicide-- which was, of course, by that time my husband was very sick with [? Parkinsonism. ?] And Michael was by that time on his own. He-- he was working. He had graduated from college.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that in the light of this-- all that went before-- I don't know if it connects or if it doesn't connect. And frankly, I don't care. It's just-- and that's how it was. And that's how it is. Ruth, I know right now, presently, you are very active in OSE.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you want to say about that before we end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- we had a big reunion in December. Now, we have always kept in touch. And I have always contributed. Every year we have contributed. And then, when we had this big reunion, and we looked at each other, and saw how old we really are, it was by common consensus-- hey, we ought to not just do it every 10 years or so. We ought to really be in touch and do something. We owe them at-- if we are here, that means we owe them our lives, our debt of gratitude. And we should let people know. Because people know all these negative aspects of these years. [INAUDIBLE] you have the OSE, which is really a-- something that they saved between 7,000 to 9,000 lives, and so on, and so forth. So they asked me if I could initiate. So I initiated. And we're working on this. And we hope-- we hope it'll be successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. I think at this point I would like you to bring your son--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into the video. And I'll ask you a few more questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- Rose, I would like it to introduce your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Michael Francis. And I'm very proud of him. He's my handsome son. And he's married. There-- the picture is behind us.

speaker: interviewer
text: His wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife's name is Cheryl. And they make a beautiful couple. And I am very, very happy with them. It's the-- it's really the anchor of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask you some questions now that you can either address to Michael the answers, or to me. Ruth, why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck. Sheer luck. The OSE-- without them-- if they had not taken me out of Le Milles, I would have been together with whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your religious beliefs have changed in any way as a result of the Holocaust-- prior to, after, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was a child, and in my mother's home, of course, I believed in God. I am sure I did. No, I do not. I do not. I do not. I, however, am very strongly Jewish-- my heritage, my history. But the theo-- theological part of-- of praying to a merciful God-- definitely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your thoughts about Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm very-- how can I put it in one sentence? I am very grateful for Israel. I am very, very grateful for Israel. Had there been an Israel in 1938-- you know, with an if, you can put Israel in a bottle. So of course, I'm very grateful for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have dreams about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's funny you ask that. When we moved from the little house to the big house in Great Neck, I had a recurrent dream. And I remember screaming. Do you remember? I would wake up with scream. And that dream was that I was holding Michael in my left arm, and I was holding Joey by the hand. And we were running down a cobblestone street. And I lost Joey. My hand was empty. And I squeezed Michael so that I could feel his ribs. I remember when he was very little, we used to count his ribs in the little pool. And I could feel in my arms his ribs. But I had-- I would wake up screaming. I had many dreams about my parents, talking with my parents. And when I would wake up from a-- a dream like that, I would be devastated. But I can't say Holocaust as such, because I was not in a concentration camp. And it was so-- as you heard. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What advice would you like to keep your son and to future generations about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: To remember. To remember. There is no-- and to-- to make-- to guard very carefully. To be truthful about it. As far as I can see, they say history repeats itself. So I will have to conclude that somehow, the Jews may be the subject of extermination once again, but that this should be a remembrance that with enough-- with a concentrated effort of resisting, bullies are nothing but cowards in disguise, really. And in the few instances of resistance, there was success. In other words, be very vigilant-- very vigilant, and not think that anti-Semitism is a [? parlor ?] thing-- that it is real, that it is here. And to do the best you can, and to be faithful to our heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, would you like to say something to your mother? You're second generation, child of survivors. Is there anything you'd like to say regarding that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, regarding what you just said, especially in terms of the-- the importance of being vigilant, I'm concerned that my generation is not vigilant enough. Out of nothing but the parental goodness of your hearts, all of you who survived, those who-- Americans who grew appear, through the Depression, who had their own difficulties-- obviously nothing that compared to yours-- wanted nothing more than to give the best to their children. And you did. And I'm concerned because of the comforts we live with, the lack of hardship, which was all you wanted for us, there's a complacency. I think my generation does not take it seriously enough. We see things happening in this country-- bullies, as you say. And we do nothing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm concerned about that. I speak up about it whenever possible. And I will continue to-- to do so. And that's why I think this is all so important. And I-- I'm so glad you've finally been given the opportunity and necessity to sit down and really tell the whole story. Because for whatever reason-- she spoke of people who choose to forget about-- well, not forget about it, but simply not talk about it. And my wife-- both my wife's parents are survivors. Her father passed away very recently. And it's terrible, because he should have been next on the list for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He had--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- I understand he had the papers to do an oral history.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did, yeah-- passed away very suddenly. But I just think it's-- we could be doing more. We could be doing more. And I'm glad we're doing this. Because this is part of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you'll help me with the OSE, you'll be doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll lick stamps.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. I think we're going to conclude the interview now. So thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a picture of my grandmother, my mother's mother, [? Liba ?] Goldman. I have this picture because I got the pictures from my uncle, her oldest son, who had come to the United States in 1933. And he had this picture of his mother. Obviously, it was taken before she was ill, when she was still well, in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. It must have been taken before 1932. This is a picture of my father and his brother. My father is the younger, shorter one. And his oth-- that's his brother, his older brother, David, who was deported from Vienna. This picture was taken when they arrived in Vienna-- shortly thereafter-- from Poland, to be with their uncle, so that my father finished school in Vienna. And I would assume he is about 11 or 12 years old. And as he was born in '93, it's before World War II.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you correct that, again?

speaker: interviewer
text: To just-- yeah, say--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it's not World War II, of course. It's before World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stand by.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father in 1927, before he was married. Because in the back is a note to his brother-in-law, who was, I suppose, already the first time in America. And he is introducing himself, because he's getting married to my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is the note on the back of the card?

speaker: interviewee
text: The back of the card is-- says, my dear brother-in-law. And it is in German, so-- but it's inscribed. And I had this picture from his brother-in-law, my uncle, who gave it to me. And here it says, [GERMAN], dear brother-in-law, [GERMAN], since you don't know me personally, I am sending you this picture, et cetera. This is my parents' wedding picture, which also was sent here. And that is how come I have it. They were married in April, 1927. And I'm very, very fond of this picture, because I was told it was a love match-- nothing arranged, like some in the older Jewish families, that it was a real love match. And I'm sure it was. Because 10 years that I spent, I know it was. A picture of me in the country. And I must have been a little over two, because it's the summertime. And I am holding my cousin Charlotte's doll, because, as I said before, I only liked dolls if they belonged to my cousin Charlotte. And that's it. This is a picture taken in 1938 in my Aunt Helen's courtyard. On my-- I'm going from right to left. And my Aunt Helen was the oldest. She's on the right. Then came my Aunt Betty, who was with us throughout the war. Then it is my mother. Then it is my Uncle Henry, and I believe-- and my Uncle Henry came to the United States. And I believe the occasion of this picture was that my Uncle Henry was about to leave. So this picture was taken in the summer of 1938. This is a letter written by my father on October 9, 1940 from Saint-Cyprien, the first camp that he was in, in France. And it is addressed to my Uncle Henry, whose was picture was on the screen before. Just to say one or two sentences of it, I would be very grateful to you if you-- you-- if you went to visit all my relatives in order to ask them for help, not so much for myself but for poor Fannie and our child. They have no means of support. Fannie looks alarmingly bad, has lost a lot of weight, and is terribly sad. They are helpless. And Fannie got sick as a result of having been imprisoned earlier. I can't bear it anymore. We abandoned everything in An-- Antwerp, left with one shirt, took nothing. On top of this, Fannie has the constant [NON-ENGLISH] of my being here.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's more. Two, three, four, five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here-- this is Chabannes. This photo was taken in Chabannes. On the right is my best friend, Angele, who now mar-- is married, is a grandmother, and who lives in Florida. It is interesting to note that we wore similar shorts and tops, which were donated by the American Quakers. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where it was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chabannes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Chabannes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And say the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. This must be 1941, when I was 13. And she's older. She was 14. This is all-- the following year, a picture of Angele and myself. Now Angele is to my-- well, on the left on the picture. And I remember, it was near a little pond, near a lake. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chabannes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Chabannes, in 1942, in early 1942, in Chabannes. And we certainly look unaware of what was going on at the time. Because indeed, we were unaware of what was going on at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were snapshots taken by whoever had a camera. Some of the counselors had cameras. Some of the older children had cameras. And they were usually developed in-- in-- in the chateau, because was there was no money to go to a photographer. So I remember, they had rigged up a darkroom to-- to develop their own pictures. When we were in [? Moutier-Salins, ?] under the protection on the combination OSE and a convent, it was-- Moutiers is a resort, a watering town, I think. And of course, the pool was available. And we went to the pool. And the boy to the right, with his arms behind his head, is-- [LAUGHS] is Alex. And he came to Switzerland with me. He lives now in Paris. I'm on the bottom row, the third one from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken in the summer of 1943-- summer after the first unsuccessful effort to go to Switzerland. So this is the summer of 1943, Moutiers [? Sur ?] [? Salin. ?] This is a photograph of the Chateau de Chabannes. I don't know why it is called Chateau. Is just a manor house. But I suppose it was once the-- a squire's manor house. It was rat infested. I never saw such huge rats before or since that were walking around there. We used to tuck ourselves into tightly with the-- with the sheets. Because we-- I mean, I was very much afraid of the rats. We planted our own vegetables, because there was a vast acreage. And we planted vegetables, in order to be as self supportive as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would this be in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well this-- it's still standing now, in 1995. So I don't know when it was built. I really don't. And what we see here is the former hothouse, [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean, how did you get the picture? I got the-- I had-- I don't know, somebody who took it. I have had the picture for a long time. I don't know whether it was taken after the war, during, before. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are in lieu of a passport. This was my trav-- these were my traveling papers, because I was not a united citizen yet. And I was stateless. I was a stateless person. And this was issued for stateless people. And even after I was married, I was not a citizen yet. And this is the paper I traveled with. I needed this, because we went on our honeymoon in 1951, to Europe. And I had no papers of any kind. And this was issued by the American government so that I could safely come back. My husband was worried that I-- he couldn't get me back into the country. This is our wedding picture. We were married in a townhouse on the East Side-- I can't remember the address-- by Rabbi Eugene [? Sack, ?] from Brooklyn. They had put on a chuppah. I guess it was the 1950 version of-- I don't know-- a small Leonard's. No, it was very nice. Of course, having no parents, I wore no white dress. And that was it. This is to prove that I came to this country-- it's a little bit out of sequence. This is the menu of the captain's dinner, dated June 28, 1946 on-- on the TES Uruguay, from the Moore-McCormack line. It was the first voyage of the Uruguay, after it had been refitted for a passenger ship, after having been a troop transport. I-- there, too, I just thought it was a sort of Hollywood on the sea. It was great luxury for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family in 1957. I am holding Michael on my lap. I-- he's probably a year-- a year old. And Joey was three and 1/2. And what can I say? If I didn't have these pictures, I would have trouble remembering those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] This is the wedding picture of my beautiful daughter-in-law, Cheryl, and my Michael, on November 23, 1987. They were married by the rabbi who bar mitzvahed Michael. So that made me feel-- made us all feel very good, a little sense of continuity. And I do hope that he will name their baby, also, someday.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a letter from the French ministry, dated 1950--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight-- verifying that I was in the camps, and that my father and mother were, and that they were deported from [? Drancy. ?] And that is the document that states this. So that's really it. That is the document. This is my grandfather Isaac Goldman's tombstone. He was the husband of [? Liba, ?] whose picture was first. The Jewish cemeteries in Vienna were never vandalized, never touched. If the stones were well built, they-- they remained. Everything remained untouched. It's just that it was overgrown with brambles and all kinds of vegetation of 50 years. And we cleared that. And we restored my grandfather's [AUDIO OUT]
